coredata.dc:title: Synergistic impact of nanoarchitectured GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) modified glassy carbon electrode in the electrochemical detection of guanine and adenine
coredata.dc:creator: Vinoth, Victor
Kaimal, Reshma
Selvamani, Muthamizh
Michael, Rubina
Pugazhenthiran, Nalandhiran
Mangalaraja, Ramalinga Viswanathan
Valdés, Héctor
Anandan, Sambandam
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facile green approach for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) embedded on silicate network silver nanocrystals (GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)) is reported. Moreover, glassy carbon-GC electrodes were modified with the prepared nanocomposite containing graphene quantum dots supported on silver nanocrystals (GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)) and applied for simultaneous detection of guanine (GA) and adenine (AD). The chemically modified electrode was assessed during the determination of purine bases by cyclic voltammetry-CV and differential pulse voltammetry-DPV. The incorporation of GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites over the surface of the GC electrode considerably enhances the anodic peak currents and decreases the adenine and guanine peak potentials. Compared to other electrodes, GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC improved the electrochemical behavior towards the detection of adenine and guanine. At optimal conditions, calibration curves were obtained by DPV being linear in the range of 0.1–6.0 μM and 0.1–5.0 μM for guanine and adenine, respectively. The detection limits of both guanine and adenine were estimated as 0.1 μM. Additionally, interferences analyses were performed on the existence of other interferent compounds. Furthermore, the method developed for the identification of GA and AD was proved using fish sperm DNA samples.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Pugazhenthiran Nalandhiran currently working as an assistant professor of chemistry at the Universidad Tecnica Federico Santa Maria, Valparaiso. Dr. N. Pugazhenthiran obtained his doctoral degree (Ph. D., in Chemistry) at the School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India. He got four research grants from ANID-FONDECYT and ANID-FONDEF, the Government of Chile from the period of November 2015 to March 2023. Moreover, he has published 32 scientific articles in WoS-indexed Journals. Additionally, he has published four book chapters and granted two patents in his credit. His research focuses on the development of nanostructured materials for energy and environmental applications.

Muthamizh Selvamani working as an Assistant Professor-Research in the Department of Physiology at Saveetha Dental College and Hospital. She pursued her Doctor of Philosophy (2013-2018) in Inorganic Chemistry at the University of Madras, Chennai, India. During her Ph.D., She was worked as Junior Research Fellow in UGC-UPE Phase-II Project and received a NAMASTE INDO-EUROPE Visiting Fellowship to work at the Department of Chemistry, Universitat Politècnica de València, Spain. The NAMASTE INDO-EUROPE Fellowship was hosted by Prof. Ramon Martinez-Manez. After the successful completion of the Doctoral thesis, Dr. Muthamizh received India’s Prestigious Fellowship called National Post-Doctoral Fellowship (NPDF) awarded (2017-2019) by the Department of Science and Technology (SERB), India. This fellowship was hosted by Prof. R. Jayavel at Crystal Growth Center, Anna University, Chennai. After the completion of NPDF, Dr. Muthamizh worked as a FONDEYT Postdoctoral Fellowship (2019-2022) in Chile, Lattin America. This fellowship was carried out at the University of Concepcion, Chile under the Guidance of Prof. David Contreras. Dr. Muthamizh’s research interest includes the Synthesis of Nanomaterials and Polymer Nanocomposites, Electro-chemical Biosensors, photocatalysts, Super-capacitor, and Hydrogen Evolution reactions. From her research outcomes, she published more than 37 papers and articles in both national and international journals with a Scopus H index of 16 and a total citation of 752.

The authors V. Vinoth and H. Valdés gratefully acknowledge the ANID/FONDECYT Post-doctoral Project No. 3190256, Government of Chile, Santiago, for the financial assistance. The author S. Anandan wants to thank SPARC funding agency (SPARC/2018-2019/P236/SL). Ms. Reshma expresses gratitude to the MHRD for funding her Ph. D. fellowship.

XRD pattern (A), UV − Vis absorption spectra (B), FTIR spectra (C) of (a) GQDs, (b) AgNCs(APTS), and (c) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) and (D) Raman spectra of GQDs (a), (b) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS).

CVs obtained during sensing 0.3 mM of GA (A) and 0.3 mM of AD (B) using (a) bare/GC, (b) GQDs/GC, (c) AgNCs(APTS)/GC, and (d) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.0) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

Successive CV curves of simultaneous detection of GA and AD with a concentration of 0.1 mM using (a) bare/GC, (b) GQDs/GC, (c) AgNCs(APTS)/GC and GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

DPV curves of sensing a mix of GA and AD with a concentration of 0.1 mM using (a) bare/GC, (b) GQDs/GC, (c) AgNCs(APTS)/GC and (d) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.0). DPV conditions: Incr E of each step: 0.004 V; Amplitude: 0.01; Pulse Width (sec) = 0.05; Sample Width (sec) = 0.02; Pulse Period (sec) = 0.5 and the scan rate: 10 mV s−1.

DPV curves of using GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC for sensing a mixture of GA and AD: (A) GA at different concentrations from 0.1 to 6 μM in the presence of 2 μM AD,(B) AD at different concentrations from 0.1 to 5 μM in the presence of 2 μM GA The insets in figures (A) and (B) show calibration curves of i-t current response (Ip) as a function of the concentration of GA and AD, respectively. Measurements were conducted in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.0). DPV conditions: Incr E of each step: 0.004 V; Amplitude: 0.01; Pulse Width (sec) = 0.05; Sample Width (sec) = 0.0167; Pulse Period (sec) = 0.5; scan rate 10 mV s−1.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a facile green approach for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) embedded on silicate network silver nanocrystals (GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)) is reported. Moreover, glassy carbon-GC electrodes were modified with the prepared nanocomposite containing graphene quantum dots supported on silver nanocrystals (GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)) and applied for simultaneous detection of guanine (GA) and adenine (AD). The chemically modified electrode was assessed during the determination of purine bases by cyclic voltammetry-CV and differential pulse voltammetry-DPV. The incorporation of GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites over the surface of the GC electrode considerably enhances the anodic peak currents and decreases the adenine and guanine peak potentials. Compared to other electrodes, GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC improved the electrochemical behavior towards the detection of adenine and guanine. At optimal conditions, calibration curves were obtained by DPV being linear in the range of 0.1–6.0 μM and 0.1–5.0 μM for guanine and adenine, respectively. The detection limits of both guanine and adenine were estimated as 0.1 μM. Additionally, interferences analyses were performed on the existence of other interferent compounds. Furthermore, the method developed for the identification of GA and AD was proved using fish sperm DNA samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Detection of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) using biosensors has increased in recent years. Clinical diagnostics, physiological and pharmacological research, as well as the understanding of the basic mechanism of genetic information, all require the detection of DNA bases [1,2]. DNA encodes genetic information with distinctive combinations of four DNA bases: guanine, adenine, thymine, and cytosine. When examining the basic components of DNA, the mole percentage of guanine is comparable to cytosine and the mole percentage of adenine is according to thymine [3]. Thus, purine bases such as adenine (AD) and guanine (GA) are two vital components that play a key role in functions such as cellular energy transmission or cell signaling. Their unusual change might be a symptom of certain diseases, such as cancer [4]. Purine bases are normally detected using conventional methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography [5], isotope dilution mass spectrometry [6], or chemiluminescence [7]; however, such methods are costly and time-consuming. Thus, simple and environmentally benign approaches to monitor the changes in DNA bases are needed. Hence, electrochemical biosensing methods have started to be used due to their rapid response, minimal expense, real-time identification, and high sensitivity in contrast to available conventional biosensing techniques [8–11].

The majority of the present electroanalytical methods for DNA identification are centered on the use of two electroactive species (AD and GA) [12]. However, owing to the delayed transfer of electrons, irreversible adsorption, and high over-potential, accurate electrochemical biosensing of AD and GA could not be achieved with bare electrodes. A different modified electrodes have been developed and employed to explore the electrochemical biosensing of purine bases in an effort to overcome these limitations [13]. Noble metal nanoparticles have been applied to modified electrodes in the past due to their unique optical, electronic, great electron transfer rate, huge surface area, catalytic properties, and exceptional stability [14,15].

Among the noble metal nanoparticles, silver (Ag) nanoparticles have high conductivity and ultrahigh electrochemical performance. The presence of silver nanoparticles extends the possibilities for the manufacturing of Ag-doped nanostructures and expands their use in biosensors [16]. The use of silica [17] and carbon-based materials as by-products can enhance the electrochemical connection between the analytes and the metal nanoparticle. Combining the carbon-based materials with metal nanoparticles has been proven to have synergistic advances in the electrocatalytic applications [18]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are zero-dimensional (0D) tightly packed honeycomb graphene nanosheets with a diameter of less than 100 nm, have been employed as an efficient material in electrochemical sensing by various chemical modifications [19]. Thus, they are an ideal probe for biosensing applications due to their high surface area, chemical inertness, low cost, water solubility, low toxicity, bio-compatibility, and electrical and optical properties [20–23].

In this work, a comparative study of electrocatalytic properties of the modified glassy carbon (GC) electrodes using the synthesized nanocomposite made of graphene quantum dots supported over silver nanocrystals (GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)) is presented. The high electrical conductivity of GQDs is advantageous for improving the conductivity of the electrode surface, further amplifying the electrochemical signal even more, and increasing the electroactive area. In order to increase the sensing capability, AgNCs are wrapped in GQDs forming a nanocomposite. Surprisingly, the synergistic impact of the nanocomposite demonstrated improved electrocatalytic activity with high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability toward guanine and adenine. The prepared sensor opened up a new avenue for the development of new, effective biological detection technologies.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid (CA), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTS), nafion (5 wt%), hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), and ethanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), adenine (AD), and guanine (GA) were bought from Alfa Aesar. Mono and disodium phosphates (99 %), Fish sperm DNA were bought from Merck. All the precursor chemicals employed were of analytical quality and employed without additional purification. All the solutions were made using Millipore water having a resistivity of at least 18.2 MΩ·cm.

In this work, an easy and efficient method to synthesize GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) was applied using a sonochemical technique. Fig. S1 displays a way of illustrating the applied synthesis procedure to obtain GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites for the concurrent electrochemical identification of GA and AD. In an initial step, GQDs were synthesized by incomplete pyrolysis, of CA, containing numerous negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [24], which contribute to the production of well-dispersed solutions (yellow color). Then, after treatment with APTS followed by hydrolysis, silicate network silver nanoparticles were produced. Therefore initially, aminosilanes are reduced to the silver metal precursor, and it’s used as the stabilizer of silver nanocrystals and interlinking to GQDs. Finally, a nanocomposite was developed by ultrasonically combining GQDs and AgNCs(APTS). The application of sonication to the reaction medium produced extensive shear forces by cavitation that helped to wrap very strongly the GQDs to the surface of AgNCs(APTS). More detailed information on all the steps involved in the applied synthesis procedure is provided below.

At 160 °C, a small beaker containing approximately 2 g of CA was placed in an oil bath. The CA began to melt after 5 min, and the color transitioned from uncolored to light yellow, then become light orange after 30 min. An aqueous solution of GQDs was produced by introducing the resultant orange-colored solution drop-wise into 120 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH while stirring vigorously [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of graphene quantum dots embedded on silicate network silver nanocrystals (GQDs-AgNCs(APTS))
ce:section.ce:para: A volume of 0.39 mL of AgNO3 (0.01 M) was sonicated for 15 min with 0.25 mL of APTS (1 M). After adding 60 µL of HCl (0.1 M) to start the hydrolysis and condensation, the reaction mixture was diluted to 3 mL with double distilled water and sonicated for additional 2.5 h. The production of AgNCs(APTS) was indicated by the presence of a clear yellow colour solution. Eventually, the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites were prepared by mixing a 1:1 vol ratio of AgNCs(APTS) and prepared GQDs under the ultrasonication at room temperature. The ultrasonic irradiation was performed out employing a Kaijo Quava mini reactor 30,110 (QR-003, lot no. SM1460009) with an oscillator of type 30,301 at 50 W and 38 kHz.

A FEI Tecnai 20 G2 instrument was used for high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded using a Specord 600S diode-array UV–visible spectrophotometer (Jena, Germany). The Horiba Raman spectrometer, Lab RAM HR Evolution, was utilized to obtain the Raman spectra. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was applied to identify the crystalline phases of the synthesized materials using a Cu-Kα radiated Bruker AXS D4 Endeavor. The Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectrometer from Thermo Scientific was used to get the infrared spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical procedures for AD and GA identification using modified GC electrode with GQDs, AgNCS(APTS), and GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)
ce:section.ce:para: A conventional three-electrode system was used for the electrochemical detection of AD and GA with the assistance of electrochemical workstation CHI 650C (Austin, DX, USA). A glassy carbon (working electrode), an Ag/AgCl/saturated KCl (reference electrode), and a platinum wire (counter electrode) comprises the three-electrode system. Studies were accessed in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with a pH of 7.0, at ambient temperature. In advance to analysis, an alumina (Al2O3) suspension with particle sizes of 1, 0.3, and 0.05 nm was used to clean the GC working electrode (3 mm diameter). It was then rinsed in Milli-Q water. Then, three differently modified GC electrodes were made. Drop-casting volumes of 10 µL of individual manufactured electrocatalysts GQDs, AgNCs(APTS), and GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) onto bare GC electrodes and allowing them to dry at ambient temperature overnight. Before conducting the electrochemical tests, each modified GC electrode was topped with a binder comprised of 2 µL of a Nafion solution and allowed it to dry.

The N-glycosidic bond is one of the most reactive covalent bonds in nucleic acids, especially when it is generated by purine bases. Purine N-glycosidic bonds are more readily cleaved than pyrimidine ones within each class of nucleic acids, much like in monomer units. The investigation of the primary structure of nucleic acids now makes extensive use of the breaking of N-glycosidic linkages. These bonds can be broken in two different ways: directly, when an acid hydrolyzes the N-glycosidic bonds, and indirectly, when the hydrolysis is preceded by alteration of certain bases that dramatically weakens the binding between those bases and the carbohydrate moiety [26]. In this regard, by cleaving the purine glycoside bonds using direct method, a mild treatment of DNA with 1 M HCl results in the selective elimination of its purine bases. To assess guanine and adenine in the fish sperm DNA, it was hydrolyzed in the following ways. 5 mg of fish sperm DNA was processed in a glass tube using 2 mL of 1 M HCl. The pH of the mixture was corrected with 2 mL of 1 M NaOH after heating for 80 min in a bath of boiling water. The solution was then diluted with 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) to 10 mL. Highly pure (greater than99 %) argon gas was utilized to deaerate the sample prior to electrochemical analysis. Millipore water that had been utilized throughout the whole evaluation [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic characteristics of GQDs, AgNCs(APTS), and GQD-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1-A and 1-B exhibit typical HR-TEM images of GQDs. In Fig. 1 -A, the TEM image indicates the size distribution of the manufactured GQDs, which have very small sheets. In the HR-TEM image of the GQDs (Fig. 1-B) can be significantly seen the d-spacing of graphene with an in-plane size of 0.23 nm with high crystalline nature, which can be associated with lattice fringes value of
11
2
¯
0
11
2
¯
0
plane. Moreover, spherical nanosheets are observed. Fig. 1-C shows the size of the GQDs using a histogram plot. The obtained well-dispersed GQDs have a uniform size with an average diameter between 3.8 and 6 nm, being consistent with the reported values in the literature [28]. Fig. 1-D shows the fast Fourier transform (FFT) spot, which demonstrates the crystallinity of the synthesized GQDs [29].

HRTEM images (Fig. 2 -A & 2-B) demonstrate the clear spherical shape of the synthesized AgNCs(APTS) with uniform-sized (∼3.5–7 nm) and smooth surfaces, which are generated as a result of reduction of Ag+ ions with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane under ultrasonic condition. The synthesized silver nanoparticles are well dispersed, and no agglomeration is noticed. In Fig. 2-B, the HR-TEM image clearly shows the perfect atomic arrangement of face-centered cubic (fcc) Ag nanocrystals. Here, the lattice fringes are clearly distinguished with the atomic planes of (1 1 1) and the d-spacing value of 0.24 nm, which indicates that the prepared NCs have a good crystalline structure. Fig. 2-C depicts the histogram of the size distribution of the AgNCs(APTS). An average size of the AgNCs(APTS) was found by evaluating 100 NPs. Furtherly, the crystalline structure of the AgNCs(APTS) was demonstrated by the SAED pattern (Fig. 2-D) with bright circular rings, accordance with (1 1 1), (2 0 0), and (2 2 0) reflections of the fcc structure of AgNCs(APTS) [30].

The morphology of synthesized GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) was identified by HR-TEM, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . It can be clearly seen that the size of the final GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) hybrid ranges between 9 and 12 nm [see inset in Fig. 3-A]. These values are larger than those obtained for bare GQDs (ca. 3.8–6 nm) and AgNCs(APTS) (ca. 3.5–7 nm). Moreover, in the GQD-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites, it can be also noticed that spherical-shaped AgNCs are enclosed by many small sized GQDs, forming a core–shell-like structure. The outer planar spacing for the lattice fringes is 0.23 nm (Fig. 3-B), corresponding to the
11
2
¯
0
lattice plane of the GQDs. The positively charged AgNCs were partially capped by APTS, which are bounded on the surface of AgNCs due to the existence of the amine (–NH2) group. The formed colloidal AgNCs(APTS) provide an improved surface for the adsorption of Ag(NH3)2 +, which further facilitate the incorporation of AgNCs onto the surface of GQDs. AgNCs(APTS) have prosperous aminosilane groups on the edge, which could have strong integration with the GQDs’ terminal carboxyl and hydroxyl groups under the electrostatic interaction [31]. Through the applied ultrasonic approach, GQDs were anchored on the surface of AgNCs(APTS), yielding GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites through aminosilanes.

The XRD patterns of (a) GQDs, (b) AgNCs(APTS), and (c) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) composites are shown in Fig. 4 (A). Due to the GQDs' compact size, a broad peak is observed at 25.4° which is related to (0 0 2) plane (JCPDS-00-001-0640). The interlayer spacing is determined to be 0.34 nm, which may indicate that the GQDs' oxidized edges were caused solely by their incredibly small size. The same significant Ag diffraction peaks appear in the spectra of AgNCs (APTS) and GQDs-AgNCs (APTS), respectively, at 38.2°, 44.5°, 64.4°, and 77.4°, which correspond to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), and (3 1 1) planes attributed to the facets of the face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice of AgNCs (JCPDS card No: 04–0783). Moreover, the spikes at 34.58° and 53.11° are ascribed to the Si (1 0 0) and (2 0 1) planes, respectively (JCPDS-01-0640). In addition, the diffraction peak depicted at a 2θ value of 25.6° affirms the effective interlinking of silver nanocrystals with GQDs [32]. The wide diffraction peaks indicate that AgNCs crystals are small. The results obtained by the XRD are in an agreement with those gathered by the TEM images.

Fig. 4(B) displays UV–visible spectra of the as-prepared dispersions of GQDs (curve (a)), AgNCs(APTS) (curve (b)) and GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) (curve (c)). GQDs have two distinctive absorption maxima (curve (a)) at 228 and 340 nm, which correspond to the π → π* transition of CC and n → π* transition of the CO bond, respectively [33]. In the case of AgNCs(APTS) (curve b)), a visible absorption maxima at 425 nm could be the result of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of formed AgNCs with the particle size of 3–7 nm [34]. This is similar to the findings made using a TEM (see Fig. 2). In curve (c), the appearance of two peaks at ∼300 and 430 nm confirmed the formation of GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposite. The peak at ∼300 nm is the result of a re-established electronic conjugation from the GQDs and the absorption peak at ∼430 nm for the GQD-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites. The localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption of Ag nanocrystals is responsible for this value, while the latter significant red shift is due to the inter-particle plasmon coupling. Compare to LSPR band of AgNCs(APTS), the absorption band of GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) is broadened and red-shifted, signifying clustering of nanoparticles (neutralization is assumed to be result by adding negative charge on GQDs and positive charge on the amine-capped silver nanocrystals) [35]. The individual particle size did not increase significantly due to the small GQDs, as revealed by TEM (Fig. 1C). The obtained aqueous dispersion was stable for several months without significant accumulations. Such results demonstrate that GQDs are efficiently anchored to the surface of AgNCs(APTS).

The FTIR spectra of synthesized nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 4(C) for GQDs, AgNCs(APTS), and GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) samples. An extensive IR band of hydroxyl (–OH) groups placed at 3430 cm−1 could be observed for GQDs. Likewise, an IR band that appears as doublet bands at ∼2920 and ∼2875 cm−1 can be attributed to symmetric and asymmetric stretching of CH vibrations, respectively [36]. Furthermore, due to the abundant of CO bonds from carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in GQDs, broadband is observed around 1000–1450 cm−1 The aromatic carbon (υCC) bond is assigned to the band registered at 1570 cm−1 [37]. As for AgNCs(APTS) (curve b), the typical vibrations of ʋNH2 with respect to AgNCs(APTS) take place at 3320 cm−1. In addition, the existence of CH is proved by the examined IR band vibrations at 2853 and 1495 cm−1 [38]. The bands related to νSiOSi and νSiOC bonds are observed at 1115, and 913 cm−1, respectively. The formation of νSiOH bond results in the IR band at 997 cm−1 [39]. The distinctive absorption bands for the bending and stretching vibrations of -ʋNH are found at 1640 and 3535 cm−1, respectively. The positively charged AgNCs were partially capped by APTS, which could be bounded on the surface of AgNCs due to the existence of the amine (–NH2) group. The observed broadening and reduction of the band are due to the intramolecular hydrogen bond of silanol and amine groups. The stretching vibration of CO at 1725 cm−1 for carboxylic in the FTIR spectrum of the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposite has nearly disappeared and new bands appear at 1685/1576 cm−1 that can be connected to υCO and δN-H of the zwitterionic bonds of COO−–NH3 + [40]. The zwitterionic COO−–NH3 + links are finally converted to amide (–NH-CO) covalent bonds that are registered at 1620 cm−1. A new stretching band appears at 1110 cm−1 for the CN bond, whereas the bands of υNH of the amide functional group are observed at 2915 and 1440 cm−1 [41,42]. AgNCs(APTS) have prosperous aminosilane groups on the edge, which could be involved in strong interactions with in the GQDs’ terminal carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Such spectroscopy evidence probes that GQDs are effectively bonded to silver nanocrystals.

The Raman spectra of pristine (a) GQDs and (b) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) composite are depicted in Fig. 4(D). The pristine GQDs exhibit two characteristic peaks attributed to the D band (1363 cm−1) and the G band (1571 cm−1). As noticed in the literature [43], the D band is due to defect-functionalities or disorder at the surface of the GQDs; whereas the G band accounts for the C sp2 atom in the hexagonal lattice originating from the first-order scattering of the Raman active E2g mode. In addition, the ID/IG ratio of GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) (0.98) is higher than that of the GQDs (0.87). These could be a consequence of the incorporation of the silanol and amine groups into the GQDs, leading to the formation of further defects and edges over the functionalizing operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing of guanine and adenine
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 demonstrates cyclic voltammogram responses of (a) bare/GC, (b) GQDs/GC, (c) AgNCs(APTS)/GC, and (d) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC modified electrodes using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a redox couple solution (1 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KCl). As it is shown in curve (b), when the GQDs/GC modified electrode is used, broad quasi-reversible redox peaks (ΔEp = 220 mV) are observed, which differs from the curve obtained for bare GC (ΔEp = 107 mV) (curve (a)). In GQDs/GC, the functional groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) may have adverse effects on the electron and mass transfer processes [44]. As a result, a clear decrease in the peak current was observed for the GQDs/GC with enlarged peak-to-peak separation. Thus, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− is inhibited from diffusing across the electrode's surface. This observation indicated the GQDs modification decreased the electron transfer rate of the bare GC, which is supported by some previous reports [45,46]. In the case of AgNCs(APTS) (curve c) onto the GC electrode, effectively-defined peak was noticed, also the peak-to-peak separation (ΔEp = 101 mV) is smaller than bare GC, and the anodic (ipa: 78.8 μA) and cathodic peak (ipc: −88.67 μA) currents of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− are higher than at the bare GC electrode (ipa: 63.78 μA and ipc: −53.2 μA). This could be attributed to high surface coverage and electrocatalysis. For GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC modified electrode (curve (d)), the peak-to-peak separation (ΔEp) is 97 mV, with a very high current values (ipa = 110 μA and ipc = −84.6 μA) are shown compared to the other electrodes, which is an indication that an electrochemical catalytic activity has been achieved.

Fig. 6 illustrates cyclic voltammogram responses of modified glassy carbon electrodes such as (a) Bare/GC, (b) GQDs/GC, (c) AgNCs(APTS)/GC, and (d) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC using 0.3 mM of guanine (Fig. 6-A) and adenine (Fig. 6-B) in PBS buffer (pH = 7). Comparing the response of all modified glassy carbon electrodes, the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC demonstrates the highest peak currents in the detections of guanine and adenine. On the reverse scan, no cathodic peaks were identified within the entire potential analyzed, suggesting an irreversible oxidation process. The peak current signals of guanine and adenine in AgNCs(APTS)/GC (curve (c)) are significantly enhanced compared to the bare GC (curve (a)) on their electrochemical response. In GQDs/GC (curve (b)), the response signals grew even more, and their oxidative potential decreased, indicating that GQDs/GC had strong electrocatalytic activity for the electrochemical identification of guanine and adenine. Furthermore, a lower oxidation potential when compared to other modified electrodes for electrocatalytic oxidation of guanine and adenine was recorded at the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC modified electrode (curve (d)), demonstrated that the oxidation potential shifts towards lower potential and the current value was the highest. Such results could be due to the large area AgNCs(APTS), providing a greater number of active sites, and the high conductivity of GQDs that contributes to reducing electron transfer resistance. This may be owe to the abundant electroactive and binding sites for the immobilization and the electron transfer of AgNCs(APTS) provided by the GQDs, which together with the excellent electrocatalytic activity of AgNCs(APTS) are responsible for the significantly improved electrochemical behaviors of GA and AD at GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) modified GC. The concurrent detection of 0.1 mM guanine and adenine in 0.1 M PBS using cyclic voltammetry with four different electrodes is shown in Fig. 7 . When it comes to the usage of bare GC (a), guanine and adenine give only a slightly broad oxidation peak at 1.2 V and CV registered shows the lowest current values. When GQDs/GC (b), and AgNCs(APTS)/GC (c) modified electrodes were applied, the guanine and adenine gave two small oxidation peaks at ∼0.77 and ∼1.12 V, respectively and the cyclic voltammogram showed very low responses. Compared to the electrochemical response on the bare GCE, the peak current signals of guanine and adenine on the GQDs/GC (b) and AgNPs(APTS)/GC (c) were enhanced significantly, indicating that both electrodes had a good electrocatalytic ability to the electrochemical reaction of guanine and adenine. However, two well-emerged oxidation peaks are existed at 0.78 and 0.1 V when the GQDs-AgNPs(APTS)/GC (d) was utilized, this might be attributable to guanine and adenine electron oxidation reactions, respectively. Meanwhile, the increment in anodic current is visible. The peak separation between the two kinds of purine bases must be larger than 200 mV for simultaneous detection, which is big enough to detect both guanine and adenine without interference. The effect of combining GQDs with AgNCs improves the catalytic activity in the electrode response, being considerably favorable in the identification of guanine and adenine bases. Thus, compared to bare GC (a), GQDs/GC (b), and AgNCs(APTS)/GC (c); the modified GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC electrode demonstrates the highest peak currents in single detections or simultaneous detections of guanine and adenine.

Additionally, the electrochemical behavior of the electrodes (a) bare/GC (b) GQDs/GC, (c) AgNCs(APTS)/GC and (d) GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC was studied using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) in the presence of a mixture of 10 μM guanine and 10 μM adenine in 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.0 and results are given in Fig. 8 . In the presence of the bare GC electrode (curve (a)), the electrocatalytic oxidation of the both the analytes does not show distinguishable peaks, which proves that it is not possible to determine these molecules simultaneously using this kind of electrode. In the cases of GQDs/GC (curve (b)) and AgNCs(APTS)/GC (curve (c)), two weak anodic peaks at the electrodes’ surface were attained for guanine and adenine at ∼0.788 and ∼1.08 V, respectively. The oxidation peak current was significantly enhanced compared to the bare GC electrode (curve (a)). The GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC electrode has better performance is observed when compared to the anodic peak current values of other electrodes (curve (d)) and the maximum peak current for the oxidation of guanine and adenine using 0.1 M PBS solution at pH. 7.0. Peak currents were increased, and anodic peak potentials of guanine (0.736 V) and adenine's (1.044 V) were shifted to less positive values indicates the modification of GC surface with GQDs and AgNCs(APTS) increases the electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of both the analytes. The large separation of peak potentials (308 mV) and high electrocatalytic activity show that GQDs-AgNCs(APTS) nanocomposites can be used to develop an electrochemical sensor to selectively determine guanine and adenine in the occurrence of other molecules, or simultaneously detect them. The oxidation peaks of both guanine and adenine were well isolated from one another, and their difference in peak potentials was as high as 308 mV, attributable to the synergistic action of AgNCs(APTS) and GQDs. All oxidation currents and potentials are summarized in Table S1. The shifting of oxidation peak potentials to less positive values and enhancement of peak currents of guanine and adenine indicated that the modification of the GC surface with the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC electrode makes it a viable alternative for electroanalytical applications. The oxidation of guanine and adenine on the surface of glassy carbon electrode followed a two-step mechanism (Fig. S2) involving the total loss of 4e− and the first 2e− oxidation step was rate-determining [47].

The electrochemical performance of GQDS-AgNCs(APTS)/GC was evaluated towards guanine and adenine detection adopting the DPV method. The concentration of guanine was detected in concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 6.0 μM in the presence of 2 μM adenine (see Fig. 9 -A); whereas the variation in the concentration of adenine was followed in the concentration range from 0.1 to 5.0 μM in the presence of 2 μM guanine (see Fig. 9-B). As it can be noticed, the oxidation peak currents of guanine and adenine increase together with the increase in the concentration values, reaching peak currents at about + 0.73 and + 1.04 V, respectively. In the individual detection of guanine in the presence of adenine, a relationship between the peak current (Ip) of guanine and the concentration was obtained by fitting to a linear regression equation (Ip(μA) = 1.333x + 6.9119, R2 = 0.9912) with a detection limit of 0.1 μM (S/N = 3). In the case of the individual detection of adenine in the presence of guanine a linear regression equation between the peak current (Ip) of adenine and the concentration of adenine was attained (Ip(μA) = 0.0551x + 4.379, R2 = 0.9899) with a detection limit of 0.1 μM (S/N = 3). Additionally, results acquired in this study were corelated with further electrochemical sensors reported in the literature that electrochemically identified guanine and adenine simultaneously (see Table S2 for supporting information). The data listed in Table S2 indicates that the GQDS-AgNCs(APTS)/GC electrode for the detection of GA and AD by DPV measurements show low detection limits, and linear ranges compared to those values reported in the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, reproducibility, long-life stability and selectivity of the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical signal’s repeatability was evaluated. For that, the concentration values of 2.0 μM were obtained for guanine and adenine with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 3.8 and 2.9 %, respectively, after five successive detections. These outstanding repeatability may be due to the antifouling properties of nafion coated GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC, which prevents fouling on the electrode surface. Moreover, the reproducibility of the modified electrode was evaluated by deciding 2.0 μM of the concentration of guanine and adenine using five different modified electrodes. The five electrodes were constructed using the same suspension of the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS). Five readings from the set achieved in RSD% of guanine and adenine at 4.2 and 5.1 %, respectively. The above RSD values indicate that the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC has good reproducibility. Additionally, the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC long-term stability was examined by observing the response of peak current for 2.0 μM of mixture of guanine and adenine (1:1). During the stability test the modified electrode was deposited in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at ambient environment. The peak current outcome of the electrodes retained 93.4 and 95.3 % of their original current seven days later also for guanine and adenine, respectively. After 3 weeks, 80 % of the current response was confined. Such results prove that the stability of the modified electrodes was sufficient for a long-life voltammetric sensor.

Furthermore, the selectivity of the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC sensor in the study of guanine (20 μM) and adenine (20 μM) was examined by analyzing the recovery percentage of DNA bases in the existence of typical interfering molecules (10 fold), such as uracil, amino acids (cysteine, serine, glutamine, and tyrosine), vitamins (niacinamide and pantothenic acid), and glucose. Interferences like this are typical in animal and plant species (Fig. S6). The rates of the changes on the current of guanine and adenine in the presence of the interferents are presented in Fig. S6 (C & D). Table S3 summarizes the percentage of DNA base recovery results (see supporting information). The DNA baseline values were recovered in 91 % despite the presence of interfering compounds. An RNA base called uracil is known to interact with adenine. The recovery of guanine and adenine was still above 96 %. The above results suggest that the GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC sensor can be applied as an alternative electrode in routine sampling analyses for the selective identification of guanine and adenine even when several types of interference-causing chemicals are present.

The implementation of as-prepared GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC was examined for the detection of guanine and adenine in HCl acid-digested fish sperm DNA. The peak currents of guanine and adenine were measured using conventional addition methods. First, 100 µL of hydrolyzed fish sperm DNA was introduced to a cell containing 20 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0), and the peak currents of guanine and adenine were measured (Fig. S7). The cell was then injected with standard guanine and adenine solutions, and the peak currents of the guanine and adenine were detected again. Distinct amounts of guanine and adenine were introduced into the real sample solution and the data were taken to determine the recovery rates. Calculations revealed that the recovery ratios for guanine and adenine were between 99 and 100.5 % and 99.6 and 101.4 %, respectively (Table S4). Such results indicate that the prepared GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC electrodes are extremely dependable and sensitive, and they may easily be used to measure GA and AD in real samples.

In this work, a novel GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC electrode was constructed and used for electrochemical sensing of guanine and adenine. A significant improvement in the values of a negative shift in the oxidation peak potentials along with the increasing oxidation peak currents in the modified electrode illustrates the great electrocatalytic ability of the electrode. Due to the synergistic effect provided by AgNCs(APTS) and GQDs, guanine and adenine both had distinct oxidation peaks that were far apart from one another. The peak potential separation was as large as 308 mV. The anodic peak currents had a linear relationship with guanine and adenine concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 6.0 μM and 0.1 to 5.0 μM, respectively, having both a detection limit of 0.1 μM (S/N = 3). The newly improved electrode was also used to detect guanine and adenine in the DNA samples of real fish sperm and acceptable results were obtained. In comparison to other electrochemical biosensors employed for the identification of guanine and adenine, the designed GQDs-AgNCs(APTS)/GC electrode exhibited superior performance over a wide linear range, with excellent stability, selectivity, and reproducibility, making it a potential candidate for its application in biological molecular analysis and clinical diagnosis.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Vinoth Victor received the BSc., MSc., and M.Phil degrees in Chemistry, in 2005, 2008, and 2010, respectively, from St. Joseph’s College, Trichy, India. He obtained his doctoral degree (Ph. D., in Chemistry) at the Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Chemistry, Trichy, India, under the supervision of Prof. S. Anandan. He is currently working as a postdoctorate fellow of Chemistry at the Universidad Tecnica Federico Santa Maria, Valparaiso. He got two research grants from ANID-FONDECYT and ANID-FONDEF, the Government of Chile from the period of November 2018 to March 2022. Moreover, he has published 15 scientific articles in WoS-indexed Journals. His research focuses on the development of chemicals and biosensors for accurate and fast detection of disease biomarkers and environmental contaminants.

Reshma Kaimal is currently a Ph.D. research scholar at the Department of Chemistry of the National Institute of Technology, Tiruchirappalli. She obtained her master’s degree in Chemistry from Calicut University, Kerala. She published 5 scientific articles in WoS indexed Journals (Rank Q1: 4, Q3:1). Her current research focuses on electrochemical sensors.

Muthamizh Selvamani working as an Assistant Professor-Research in the Department of Physiology at Saveetha Dental College and Hospital. She pursued her Doctor of Philosophy (2013-2018) in Inorganic Chemistry at the University of Madras, Chennai, India. During her Ph.D., She was worked as Junior Research Fellow in UGC-UPE Phase-II Project and received a NAMASTE INDO-EUROPE Visiting Fellowship to work at the Department of Chemistry, Universitat Politècnica de València, Spain. The NAMASTE INDO-EUROPE Fellowship was hosted by Prof. Ramon Martinez-Manez. After the successful completion of the Doctoral thesis, Dr. Muthamizh received India’s Prestigious Fellowship called National Post-Doctoral Fellowship (NPDF) awarded (2017-2019) by the Department of Science and Technology (SERB), India. This fellowship was hosted by Prof. R. Jayavel at Crystal Growth Center, Anna University, Chennai. After the completion of NPDF, Dr. Muthamizh worked as a FONDEYT Postdoctoral Fellowship (2019-2022) in Chile, Lattin America. This fellowship was carried out at the University of Concepcion, Chile under the Guidance of Prof. David Contreras. Dr. Muthamizh’s research interest includes the Synthesis of Nanomaterials and Polymer Nanocomposites, Electro-chemical Biosensors, photocatalysts, Super-capacitor, and Hydrogen Evolution reactions. From her research outcomes, she published more than 37 papers and articles in both national and international journals with a Scopus H index of 16 and a total citation of 752.

Rubina Michael graduated with B.E in Electronics & Communication Engineering from Vivekanandha Institute of Engineering and Technology for women in Tiruchengode under Anna University, Chennai. She has obtained a Master of Engineering in Embedded System Technologies in KSR College of Engineering under Anna University, Chennai. She has published 5 papers in standard International and UGC-approved Journals. She also published 4 papers at International Conferences. Her key areas of research interest are Wireless Sensor Networks, Artificial Intelligence, Sensor Technology, IoT & Digital signal processing. She has 3 years of teaching experience and guided many academic projects in the field of wireless sensor networks, wearable devices & IoT. she is currently working as an Assistant professor at Narasu’s Sarathy Institute of Technology, Salem, Tamilnadu, India.

Pugazhenthiran Nalandhiran currently working as an assistant professor of chemistry at the Universidad Tecnica Federico Santa Maria, Valparaiso. Dr. N. Pugazhenthiran obtained his doctoral degree (Ph. D., in Chemistry) at the School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India. He got four research grants from ANID-FONDECYT and ANID-FONDEF, the Government of Chile from the period of November 2015 to March 2023. Moreover, he has published 32 scientific articles in WoS-indexed Journals. Additionally, he has published four book chapters and granted two patents in his credit. His research focuses on the development of nanostructured materials for energy and environmental applications.

Mangalaraja Ramalinga Viswanathan is the Professor in the Faculty of Engineering and Sciences, Universidad Adolfo Ibáñez, Santiago. Previously, he served as the Full Professor in the Department of Materials Engineering, at the University of Concepción in Concepción, Chile. He worked as the Director of International Relations at the University of Concepción for a period of two years from 2018 to 2019. He served as the Director of the Department of Materials Engineering for a period of six years from 2010 to 2016. He also served as the President of the Chilean Metallurgy and Materials Society (SOCHIMM) for the period of 2015-2017. Also, member of The Mineral, Metals and Materials Society (TMS) and Singapore Materials Research Society (MRS-S); and American (ACerS) and PAN American Ceramic Societies. His primary research interests focus on nanotechnology-driven advanced materials (functional materials for energy and environmental applications) and the micro-nano-structure-property relationship of functional and structural ceramics and metallic composite materials. He authored/co-authored over 260 research articles in the refereed international scientific indexing (ISI) journal publications, 27 book chapters, three patents, one of the Editors in two Books, and more than 180 research presentations in several International Conferences/Seminars.

Héctor Valdés is the professor at Universidad Católica de la Santísima Concepción. He received his Ph.D. in Chemical Engineering at Universidad de Concepción, Chile, in 2003. His main areas of research are related to the development of new technologies for the control of atmospheric pollution, non-conventional technologies for water and wastewater treatment, and the development of new materials for biosensing applications, using activated carbons, zeolites, and graphene nanosheets and quantum dots modified with metal and oxide metal nanoparticles and bismuth oxyhalides. Currently, he is the author of 77 Scopus articles (57 indexed in WoS), with 1790 Scopus citations and an H-index of 20 (Scopus, WoS). He has 2 invention patents.

Sambandam Anandan obtained his doctoral degree in chemistry from the University of Madras, India under the supervision of Prof. P. Maruthamuthu, where he worked on Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells. After two postdoctoral terms at the Chungnam National University in South Korea (under Prof. Minjoong Yoon) and Hong Kong University of Science & Technology in Hong Kong (under Prof. Yang Shehe), he worked as a visiting researcher at the National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST) in Japan under (Dr. Masaharu Okazaki). Subsequently, he joined the Central Electrochemical Research Institute in India and later the National Institute of Technology, Tiruchirappalli, where he is now a Professor of Physical Chemistry, leading the research group “Nanomaterials and Solar Energy Conversion Processes”. His recent research interests include hybrid semiconductor nanomaterials and their applications in Dye-sensitized solar cells, perovskite solar cells, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, supercapacitors, fuel cells, and biosensors. He is the author of ca. 360 research articles and 2 international patents. He is serving as an editorial board member in Ultrasonics Sonochemistry (Elsevier Journal).
